scenario: Delta Bank's Spring SDK job worker handles 9000 jobs per day in Camunda 8.8 for the type charge-payment. The method returns a map of variables, and the team wants the framework to call complete-job automatically on a successful return rather than requiring an explicit completion call.
question: Which @JobWorker attribute controls whether the SDK auto-completes the job on a successful method return?

options.text: Set autoComplete = true on the @JobWorker annotation; the SDK calls complete-job with the returned map on successful return
Set autoActivate = true on the @JobWorker annotation; the SDK calls activate-job in advance and complete-job on every method return path
Return null from the method; the SDK interprets a null return as a signal to call complete-job with no variables on the activated job
Set fetchVariables = {} on the @JobWorker annotation; the SDK reads the empty set as an opt-in for auto-completion of jobs of the type

a.text: Correct. The @JobWorker annotation exposes autoComplete; when true, the SDK invokes complete-job with the returned variables on success.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no autoActivate attribute; activation is always handled by the SDK runtime.

c.text: Incorrect. Returning null does not implicitly switch on auto-completion; the explicit attribute controls it.

d.text: Incorrect. fetchVariables is a filter for which variables the worker receives, not an auto-completion flag.

explanation: @JobWorker(autoComplete = true) makes the Spring SDK call complete-job with the returned variables on a successful method return.
